title_en: The Objectives of the Shari'ah in Legal Reasoning
content_en:
Ibn al-Qayyim's treatment of the higher objectives of Islamic law represents one of the most philosophically rich sections of I'lam al-Muwaqqi'in. He articulates the classical framework of the maqasid al-Shariah: the Shariah was revealed to preserve and promote five essential goods for human beings, namely religion, life, intellect, lineage, and wealth. These five are not inventions of the jurists; they emerge from a careful survey of the rulings of the Quran and Sunnah, which consistently direct human conduct in ways that protect these goods and prohibit conduct that harms or destroys them. Understanding this framework is not optional for the jurist; it is essential, because without it the jurist cannot understand why the rules exist and therefore cannot apply them correctly when circumstances become complex.

The connection between maqasid and legal reasoning works in two directions. In the first direction, understanding the objective of a ruling helps the jurist extend it correctly to new cases: if he knows that a prohibition exists to protect public health, he can identify new practices that threaten public health and apply the prohibition's logic to them even if the original text did not mention them explicitly. In the second direction, understanding the objective of a ruling protects against its misapplication: if a literal reading of a rule in a particular situation would produce an outcome that defeats the very objective the rule was meant to serve, the jurist must recognize that the literal reading is incorrect and look more carefully at what the text actually requires. Ibn al-Qayyim gives numerous examples of both types from the classical literature.

Ibn al-Qayyim's concern with maqasid is inseparable from his critique of legal stratagems (hiyal). Many such stratagems involved technically satisfying the formal conditions of a ruling while entirely defeating its purpose. A contract engineered to produce the economic effect of an interest-bearing loan while avoiding the formal definition of riba satisfies the letter of the prohibition while violating its spirit completely. Ibn al-Qayyim argues that such maneuvers are not legally valid because they fail the test that the Shariah itself sets: Allah and His Messenger look to intentions and purposes, not merely outer forms. A contract whose purpose is to produce a prohibited outcome is a prohibited contract regardless of how its formal elements are arranged. The maqasid framework provides the analytical basis for this critique.

Beyond the immediate jurisprudential applications, Ibn al-Qayyim's maqasid discussion reflects a broader conviction about the nature of Islamic law: it is not an arbitrary set of commands and prohibitions but a coherent system designed with the welfare of human beings in view. This does not mean that human welfare calculations override explicit texts; the texts are the authoritative expression of what Allah knows to constitute genuine human welfare, which may not always match what humans calculate for themselves. But it does mean that the jurist who understands the objectives of the law is better equipped to apply it correctly than one who treats it as a purely formal system of rules divorced from any purpose. Ibn al-Qayyim sees this understanding as one of the marks that distinguishes the genuine scholar from the mere technician of legal forms.
content_id:
Pembahasan Ibnu Al-Qayyim tentang tujuan-tujuan lebih tinggi dari hukum Islam mewakili salah satu bagian yang paling kaya secara filosofis dari I'lam Al-Muwaqqiin. Beliau mengartikulasikan kerangka klasik maqasid asy-syariah: syariat diturunkan untuk melestarikan dan memajukan lima kebaikan esensial bagi manusia, yaitu agama, jiwa, akal, keturunan, dan harta. Kelima hal ini bukan temuan para ahli fikih; mereka muncul dari survei yang cermat terhadap hukum-hukum Al-Quran dan Sunnah, yang secara konsisten mengarahkan perilaku manusia dengan cara-cara yang melindungi kebaikan-kebaikan ini dan melarang perilaku yang merugikan atau menghancurkannya. Memahami kerangka ini bukan pilihan bagi ahli fikih; itu sangat penting, karena tanpanya ahli fikih tidak dapat memahami mengapa aturan-aturan itu ada dan karenanya tidak dapat menerapkannya dengan benar ketika keadaan menjadi kompleks.

Hubungan antara maqasid dan penalaran hukum bekerja dalam dua arah. Dalam arah pertama, memahami tujuan suatu hukum membantu ahli fikih memperluas hukum itu dengan benar ke kasus-kasus baru: jika ia mengetahui bahwa suatu larangan ada untuk melindungi kesehatan publik, ia dapat mengidentifikasi praktik-praktik baru yang mengancam kesehatan publik dan menerapkan logika larangan itu kepada mereka meskipun teks asli tidak menyebutkan mereka secara eksplisit. Dalam arah kedua, memahami tujuan suatu hukum melindungi dari penerapannya yang keliru: jika pembacaan literal suatu aturan dalam situasi tertentu akan menghasilkan hasil yang mengalahkan tujuan persis yang dimaksudkan aturan itu untuk dilayani, ahli fikih harus menyadari bahwa pembacaan literal itu salah dan melihat lebih cermat apa yang sebenarnya dituntut teks itu. Ibnu Al-Qayyim memberikan banyak contoh dari kedua tipe itu dari literatur klasik.

Kepedulian Ibnu Al-Qayyim terhadap maqasid tidak terpisahkan dari kritiknya terhadap hiyal (rekayasa hukum). Banyak hiyal semacam itu melibatkan pemenuhan syarat-syarat formal suatu hukum secara teknis sambil sepenuhnya mengalahkan tujuannya. Kontrak yang dirancang untuk menghasilkan efek ekonomi dari pinjaman berbunga sambil menghindari definisi formal riba memenuhi bunyi larangan itu sambil sepenuhnya melanggar spiritnya. Ibnu Al-Qayyim berpendapat bahwa manuver-manuver semacam itu tidak sah secara hukum karena gagal dalam ujian yang ditetapkan oleh syariat itu sendiri: Allah dan Rasul-Nya melihat niat-niat dan tujuan-tujuan, bukan sekadar bentuk-bentuk lahiriah. Kontrak yang tujuannya adalah menghasilkan hasil yang dilarang adalah kontrak yang dilarang terlepas dari bagaimana elemen-elemen formalnya disusun. Kerangka maqasid menyediakan dasar analitis untuk kritik ini.

Di luar penerapan-penerapan fikih yang langsung, diskusi maqasid Ibnu Al-Qayyim mencerminkan keyakinan yang lebih luas tentang hakikat hukum Islam: ia bukan seperangkat perintah dan larangan yang sewenang-wenang melainkan sistem yang koheren yang dirancang dengan mempertimbangkan kesejahteraan manusia. Ini tidak berarti bahwa kalkulasi-kalkulasi kesejahteraan manusia mengesampingkan teks-teks eksplisit; teks-teks itu adalah ekspresi otoritatif dari apa yang Allah ketahui sebagai kesejahteraan manusia yang sejati, yang mungkin tidak selalu sesuai dengan apa yang manusia hitung untuk dirinya sendiri. Tetapi itu berarti bahwa ahli fikih yang memahami tujuan-tujuan hukum lebih siap untuk menerapkannya dengan benar daripada yang memperlakukannya sebagai sistem formal aturan yang murni yang terpisah dari tujuan apa pun. Ibnu Al-Qayyim melihat pemahaman ini sebagai salah satu tanda yang membedakan ulama sejati dari sekadar teknisi bentuk-bentuk hukum.
content_ar:
Ibn al-Qayyim's treatment of the higher objectives of Islamic law represents one of the most philosophically rich sections of I'lam al-Muwaqqi'in. He articulates the classical framework of the maqasid al-Shariah: the Shariah was revealed to preserve and promote five essential goods for human beings, namely religion, life, intellect, lineage, and wealth. These five are not inventions of the jurists; they emerge from a careful survey of the rulings of the القرآن الكريم and السنة النبوية, which consistently direct human conduct in ways that protect these goods and prohibit conduct that harms or destroys them. Understanding this framework is not optional for the jurist; it is essential, because without it the jurist cannot understand why the rules exist and therefore cannot apply them correctly when circumstances become complex.

The connection between maqasid and legal reasoning works in two directions. In the first direction, understanding the objective of a ruling helps the jurist extend it correctly to new cases: if he knows that a prohibition exists to protect public health, he can identify new practices that threaten public health and apply the prohibition's logic to them even if the original text did not mention them explicitly. In the second direction, understanding the objective of a ruling protects against its misapplication: if a literal reading of a rule in a particular situation would produce an outcome that defeats the very objective the rule was meant to serve, the jurist must recognize that the literal reading is incorrect and look more carefully at what the text actually requires. Ibn al-Qayyim's concern with maqasid is inseparable from his critique of legal stratagems (hiyal). Many such stratagems involved technically satisfying the formal conditions of a ruling while entirely defeating its purpose. A contract engineered to produce the economic effect of an interest-bearing loan while avoiding the formal definition of riba satisfies the letter of the prohibition while violating its spirit completely. Ibn al-Qayyim argues that such maneuvers are not legally valid because they fail the test that the Shariah itself sets: الله and His Messenger look to intentions and purposes, not merely outer forms. A contract whose purpose is to produce a prohibited outcome is a prohibited contract regardless of how its formal elements are arranged. The maqasid framework provides the analytical basis for this critique.

Beyond the immediate jurisprudential applications, Ibn al-Qayyim's maqasid discussion reflects a broader conviction about the nature of Islamic law: it is not an arbitrary set of commands and prohibitions but a coherent system designed with the welfare of human beings in view. This does not mean that human welfare calculations override explicit texts; the texts are the authoritative expression of what الله knows to constitute genuine human welfare, which may not always match what humans calculate for themselves. Ibn al-Qayyim sees this understanding as one of the marks that distinguishes the genuine scholar from the mere technician of legal forms.
